architecture.summary: A clinical decision support system that combines retrieval-augmented generation (RAG) with large language models and document retrieval to support diagnostic reasoning. The system retrieves medical knowledge from a document collection and augments LLM generation with retrieved context. Multiple retrieval strategies are evaluated: dense retrieval (DPR), sparse retrieval (BM25), hybrid fusion (RRF combining dense and sparse), graph-based, multimodal, self-reflective (iterative verification loops), adaptive (confidence-based), and security-focused variants. Hallucination mitigation is achieved through retrieval confidence thresholds, chain-of-thought verification, and external fact-checking. The system is designed for on-premises deployment with encryption, provenance tagging, and audit trails.

biblio.title: Evaluating Retrieval-Augmented Generation Variants for Clinical Decision Support: Hallucination Mitigation and Secure On-Premises Deployment

claims.statement: Best retrieval accuracy achieved by Haystack pipeline (DPR + BM25 + cross-encoder) and hybrid fusion (RRF) with P@5 ≥ 0.68, nDCG@10 ≥ 0.67
Sparse retrieval gave the fastest response of 120 ms
Sparse retrieval was not as accurate despite providing fastest response time
Hybrid fusion (RRF) combining dense and sparse retrieval achieved among the best retrieval accuracy results
Proposed framework for reducing hallucinations includes retrieval confidence thresholds, chain-of-thought verification, and outside fact-checking
Secure on-premises deployment incorporates encryption, provenance tagging, and audit trails
Evaluated twelve different types of RAG: dense, sparse, hybrid, graph-based, multimodal, self-reflective, adaptive, and security-focused pipelines
